summary: Gruber reviews Apple's 'Loop You In' event, pushing back against the 'That's it?' reaction by noting the Town Hall venue signaled modest expectations. He's enthusiastic about the iPhone SE as effectively a 5S-sized phone with 6S internals, calling it his likely daily driver. The 9.7-inch iPad Pro impresses with its display but he notes Apple's 'PC replacement' positioning implicitly challenges the Mac too. He touches on watch bands, renewable energy messaging, Jony Ive's absence, and Apple's 40th anniversary video.
key_points: The iPhone SE delivers near-6S specs in a 5S body, with trade-offs limited to no 3D Touch, slower Touch ID, and a lesser front camera
The 9.7-inch iPad Pro actually surpasses the 12.9-inch model in display and camera technology, not just shrinks it
Apple's 'PC replacement' pitch for the iPad Pro applies as much to the Mac as it does to Windows PCs, an uncomfortable implication
The Town Hall venue itself was the signal that nothing spectacular was coming, making the underwhelmed reaction unreasonable
Apple's renewable energy messaging would be stronger framed as proof-it-can-be-done rather than claiming to address climate change directly
money_quotes: I detect an undercurrent of 'That's it?' in the collective response to today's event, but I'm not sure what Apple could have done differently.
It's not reasonable to expect the spectacular from every single event.
I'm all for nostalgia, but I for one will not miss Town Hall. The seats are incredibly cramped, and the seat backs are hard.
I honestly think this is the phone I'm going to use for the next six months.
When Apple says 'PC' they mean 'Windows PC', but the argument applies just as much to the Mac.
It comes with a notable aesthetic price, however: an iPhone 6-style camera bump. Gross.
If you see one you like, you'd better buy it, because it might be gone in six months.
It'd be better if they simply held up their use of renewable energy as proof that it can be done, and as a challenge to other Fortune 500 companies to follow.
key_insight: The iPhone SE represents Apple's acknowledgment that the 4-inch form factor still has a devoted audience, and by packing 6S-class internals into a 5S body, they've made the most compelling small phone possible — while the iPad Pro's 'PC replacement' pitch quietly threatens the Mac as much as Windows.
